{
  "markdown": "# Claudeception\n\nEvery time you use an AI coding agent, it starts from zero. You spend an hour debugging some obscure error, the agent figures it out, session ends. Next time you hit the same issue? Another hour.\n\nThis skill fixes that. When Claude Code discovers something non-obvious (a debugging technique, a workaround, some project-specific pattern), it saves that knowledge as a new skill. Next time a similar problem comes up, the skill gets loaded automatically.\n\n## Installation\n\n### Step 1: Clone the skill\n\n**User-level (recommended)**\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\ngit clone https://github.com/blader/Claudeception.git ~/.claude/skills/claudeception\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n**Project-level**\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\ngit clone https://github.com/blader/Claudeception.git .claude/skills/claudeception\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Step 2: Set up the activation hook (recommended)\n\nThe skill can activate via semantic matching, but a hook ensures it evaluates every session for extractable knowledge.\n\n#### User-level setup (recommended)\n\n1. Create the hooks directory and copy the script:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\nmkdir -p ~/.claude/hooks\ncp ~/.claude/skills/claudeception/scripts/claudeception-activator.sh ~/.claude/hooks/\nchmod +x ~/.claude/hooks/claudeception-activator.sh\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n2. Add the hook to your global Claude settings (&#96;~/.claude/settings.json&#96;):\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;json\n{\n  \"hooks\": {\n    \"UserPromptSubmit\": [\n      {\n        \"hooks\": [\n          {\n            \"type\": \"command\",\n            \"command\": \"~/.claude/hooks/claudeception-activator.sh\"\n          }\n        ]\n      }\n    ]\n  }\n}\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n#### Project-level setup\n\n1. Create the hooks directory inside your project and copy the script:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\nmkdir -p .claude/hooks\ncp .claude/skills/claudeception/scripts/claudeception-activator.sh .claude/hooks/\nchmod +x .claude/hooks/claudeception-activator.sh\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n2. Add the hook to your project settings (&#96;.claude/settings.json&#96; in the repo):\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;json\n{\n  \"hooks\": {\n    \"UserPromptSubmit\": [\n      {\n        \"hooks\": [\n          {\n            \"type\": \"command\",\n            \"command\": \".claude/hooks/claudeception-activator.sh\"\n          }\n        ]\n      }\n    ]\n  }\n}\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nIf you already have a &#96;settings.json&#96;, merge the &#96;hooks&#96; configuration into it.\n\nThe hook injects a reminder on every prompt that tells Claude to evaluate whether the current task produced extractable knowledge. This achieves higher activation rates than relying on semantic description matching alone.\n\n## Usage\n\n### Automatic Mode\n\nThe skill activates automatically when Claude Code:\n- Just completed debugging and discovered a non-obvious solution\n- Found a workaround through investigation or trial-and-error\n- Resolved an error where the root cause wasn't immediately apparent\n- Learned project-specific patterns or configurations through investigation\n- Completed any task where the solution required meaningful discovery\n\n### Explicit Mode\n\nTrigger a learning retrospective:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n/claudeception\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nOr explicitly request skill extraction:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nSave what we just learned as a skill\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### What Gets Extracted\n\nNot every task produces a skill. It only extracts knowledge that required actual discovery (not just reading docs), will help with future tasks, has clear trigger conditions, and has been verified to work.\n\n## Research\n\nThe idea comes from academic work on skill libraries for AI agents.\n\n[Voyager](https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.16291) (Wang et al., 2023) showed that game-playing agents can build up libraries of reusable skills over time, and that this helps them avoid re-learning things they already figured out. [CASCADE](https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.23880) (2024) introduced \"meta-skills\" (skills for acquiring skills), which is what this is. [SEAgent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.04700) (2025) showed agents can learn new software environments through trial and error, which inspired the retrospective feature. [Reflexion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.11366) (Shinn et al., 2023) showed that self-reflection helps.\n\nAgents that persist what they learn do better than agents that start fresh.\n\n## How It Works\n\nClaude Code has a native skills system. At startup, it loads skill names and descriptions (about 100 tokens each). When you're working, it matches your current context against those descriptions and pulls in relevant skills.\n\nBut this retrieval system can be written to, not just read from. So when this skill notices extractable knowledge, it writes a new skill with a description optimized for future retrieval.\n\nThe description matters a lot. \"Helps with database problems\" won't match anything useful. \"Fix for PrismaClientKnownRequestError in serverless\" will match when someone hits that error.\n\nMore on the skills architecture [here](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/equipping-agents-for-the-real-world-with-agent-skills).\n\n## Skill Format\n\nExtracted skills are markdown files with YAML frontmatter:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\n---\nname: prisma-connection-pool-exhaustion\ndescription: |\n  Fix for PrismaClientKnownRequestError: Too many database connections \n  in serverless environments (Vercel, AWS Lambda). Use when connection \n  count errors appear after ~5 concurrent requests.\nauthor: Claude Code\nversion: 1.0.0\ndate: 2024-01-15\n---\n\n# Prisma Connection Pool Exhaustion\n\n## Problem\n[What this skill solves]\n\n## Context / Trigger Conditions\n[Exact error messages, symptoms, scenarios]\n\n## Solution\n[Step-by-step fix]\n\n## Verification\n[How to confirm it worked]\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nSee &#96;resources/skill-template.md&#96; for the full template.\n\n## Quality Gates\n\nThe skill is picky about what it extracts. If something is just a documentation lookup, or only useful for this one case, or hasn't actually been tested, it won't create a skill. Would this actually help someone who hits this problem in six months? If not, no skill.\n\n## Examples\n\nSee &#96;examples/&#96; for sample skills:\n\n- &#96;nextjs-server-side-error-debugging/&#96;: errors that don't show in browser console\n- &#96;prisma-connection-pool-exhaustion/&#96;: the \"too many connections\" serverless problem\n- &#96;typescript-circular-dependency/&#96;: detecting and fixing import cycles\n\n## Contributing\n\nContributions welcome. Fork, make changes, submit a PR.\n\n## License\n\nMIT\n",
  "url": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/blader/Claudeception/main/README.md"
}
```

{
  "markdown": "---\nname: claudeception\ndescription: |\n Claudeception is a continuous learning system that extracts reusable knowledge from work sessions.\n Triggers: (1) /claudeception command to review session learnings, (2) \"save this as a skill\"\n or \"extract a skill from this\", (3) \"what did we learn?\", (4) After any task involving\n non-obvious debugging, workarounds, or trial-and-error discovery. Creates new Claude Code\n skills when valuable, reusable knowledge is identified.\nauthor: Claude Code\nversion: 3.0.0\nallowed-tools:\n - Read\n - Write\n - Edit\n - Grep\n - Glob\n - WebSearch\n - WebFetch\n - Skill\n - AskUserQuestion\n - TodoWrite\n---\n\n# Claudeception\n\nYou are Claudeception: a continuous learning system that extracts reusable knowledge from work sessions and \ncodifies it into new Claude Code skills. This enables autonomous improvement over time.\n\n## Core Principle: Skill Extraction\n\nWhen working on tasks, continuously evaluate whether the current work contains extractable \nknowledge worth preserving. Not every task produces a skill—be selective about what's truly \nreusable and valuable.\n\n## When to Extract a Skill\n\nExtract a skill when you encounter:\n\n1. **Non-obvious Solutions**: Debugging techniques, workarounds, or solutions that required \n significant investigation and wouldn't be immediately apparent to someone facing the same \n problem.\n\n2. **Project-Specific Patterns**: Conventions, configurations, or architectural decisions \n specific to this codebase that aren't documented elsewhere.\n\n3. **Tool Integration Knowledge**: How to properly use a specific tool, library, or API in \n ways that documentation doesn't cover well.\n\n4. **Error Resolution**: Specific error messages and their actual root causes/fixes, \n especially when the error message is misleading.\n\n5. **Workflow Optimizations**: Multi-step processes that can be streamlined or patterns \n that make common tasks more efficient.\n\n## Skill Quality Criteria\n\nBefore extracting, verify the knowledge meets these criteria:\n\n- **Reusable**: Will this help with future tasks? (Not just this one instance)\n- **Non-trivial**: Is this knowledge that requires discovery, not just documentation lookup?\n- **Specific**: Can you describe the exact trigger conditions and solution?\n- **Verified**: Has this solution actually worked, not just theoretically?\n\n## Extraction Process\n\n### Step 1: Check for Existing Skills\n\n**Goal:** Find related skills before creating. Decide: update or create new.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;sh\n# Skill directories (project-first, then user-level)\nSKILL_DIRS=(\n  \".claude/skills\"\n  \"$HOME/.claude/skills\"\n  \"$HOME/.codex/skills\"\n  # Add other tool paths as needed\n)\n\n# List all skills\nrg --files -g 'SKILL.md' \"${SKILL_DIRS[@]}\" 2\u003e/dev/null\n\n# Search by keywords\nrg -i \"keyword1|keyword2\" \"${SKILL_DIRS[@]}\" 2\u003e/dev/null\n\n# Search by exact error message\nrg -F \"exact error message\" \"${SKILL_DIRS[@]}\" 2\u003e/dev/null\n\n# Search by context markers (files, functions, config keys)\nrg -i \"getServerSideProps|next.config.js|prisma.schema\" \"${SKILL_DIRS[@]}\" 2\u003e/dev/null\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n| Found | Action |\n|--------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|\n| Nothing related | Create new |\n| Same trigger and same fix | Update existing (e.g., &#96;version: 1.0.0&#96; → &#96;1.1.0&#96;) |\n| Same trigger, different root cause | Create new, add &#96;See also:&#96; links both ways |\n| Partial overlap (same domain, different trigger) | Update existing with new \"Variant\" subsection |\n| Same domain, different problem | Create new, add &#96;See also: [skill-name]&#96; in Notes |\n| Stale or wrong | Mark deprecated in Notes, add replacement link |\n\n**Versioning:** patch = typos/wording, minor = new scenario, major = breaking changes or deprecation.\n\nIf multiple matches, open the closest one and compare Problem/Trigger Conditions before deciding.\n\n### Step 2: Identify the Knowledge\n\nAnalyze what was learned:\n- What was the problem or task?\n- What was non-obvious about the solution?\n- What would someone need to know to solve this faster next time?\n- What are the exact trigger conditions (error messages, symptoms, contexts)?\n\n### Step 3: Research Best Practices (When Appropriate)\n\nBefore creating the skill, search the web for current information when:\n\n**Always search for:**\n- Technology-specific best practices (frameworks, libraries, tools)\n- Current documentation or API changes\n- Common patterns or solutions for similar problems\n- Known gotchas or pitfalls in the problem domain\n- Alternative approaches or solutions\n\n**When to search:**\n- The topic involves specific technologies, frameworks, or tools\n- You're uncertain about current best practices\n- The solution might have changed after January 2025 (knowledge cutoff)\n- There might be official documentation or community standards\n- You want to verify your understanding is current\n\n**When to skip searching:**\n- Project-specific internal patterns unique to this codebase\n- Solutions that are clearly context-specific and wouldn't be documented\n- Generic programming concepts that are stable and well-understood\n- Time-sensitive situations where the skill needs to be created immediately\n\n**Search strategy:**\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n1. Search for official documentation: \"[technology] [feature] official docs 2026\"\n2. Search for best practices: \"[technology] [problem] best practices 2026\"\n3. Search for common issues: \"[technology] [error message] solution 2026\"\n4. Review top results and incorporate relevant information\n5. Always cite sources in a \"References\" section of the skill\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n**Example searches:**\n- \"Next.js getServerSideProps error handling best practices 2026\"\n- \"Claude Code skill description semantic matching 2026\"\n- \"React useEffect cleanup patterns official docs 2026\"\n\n**Integration with skill content:**\n- Add a \"References\" section at the end of the skill with source URLs\n- Incorporate best practices into the \"Solution\" section\n- Include warnings about deprecated patterns in the \"Notes\" section\n- Mention official recommendations where applicable\n\n### Step 4: Structure the Skill\n\nCreate a new skill with this structure:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;markdown\n---\nname: [descriptive-kebab-case-name]\ndescription: |\n  [Precise description including: (1) exact use cases, (2) trigger conditions like \n  specific error messages or symptoms, (3) what problem this solves. Be specific \n  enough that semantic matching will surface this skill when relevant.]\nauthor: [original-author or \"Claude Code\"]\nversion: 1.0.0\ndate: [YYYY-MM-DD]\n---\n\n# [Skill Name]\n\n## Problem\n[Clear description of the problem this skill addresses]\n\n## Context / Trigger Conditions  \n[When should this skill be used? Include exact error messages, symptoms, or scenarios]\n\n## Solution\n[Step-by-step solution or knowledge to apply]\n\n## Verification\n[How to verify the solution worked]\n\n## Example\n[Concrete example of applying this skill]\n\n## Notes\n[Any caveats, edge cases, or related considerations]\n\n## References\n[Optional: Links to official documentation, articles, or resources that informed this skill]\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Step 5: Write Effective Descriptions\n\nThe description field is critical for skill discovery. Include:\n\n- **Specific symptoms**: Exact error messages, unexpected behaviors\n- **Context markers**: Framework names, file types, tool names\n- **Action phrases**: \"Use when...\", \"Helps with...\", \"Solves...\"\n\nExample of a good description:\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\ndescription: |\n  Fix for \"ENOENT: no such file or directory\" errors when running npm scripts \n  in monorepos. Use when: (1) npm run fails with ENOENT in a workspace, \n  (2) paths work in root but not in packages, (3) symlinked dependencies \n  cause resolution failures. Covers node_modules resolution in Lerna, \n  Turborepo, and npm workspaces.\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Step 6: Save the Skill\n\nSave new skills to the appropriate location:\n\n- **Project-specific skills**: &#96;.claude/skills/[skill-name]/SKILL.md&#96;\n- **User-wide skills**: &#96;~/.claude/skills/[skill-name]/SKILL.md&#96;\n\nInclude any supporting scripts in a &#96;scripts/&#96; subdirectory if the skill benefits from \nexecutable helpers.\n\n## Retrospective Mode\n\nWhen &#96;/claudeception&#96; is invoked at the end of a session:\n\n1. **Review the Session**: Analyze the conversation history for extractable knowledge\n2. **Identify Candidates**: List potential skills with brief justifications\n3. **Prioritize**: Focus on the highest-value, most reusable knowledge\n4. **Extract**: Create skills for the top candidates (typically 1-3 per session)\n5. **Summarize**: Report what skills were created and why\n\n## Self-Reflection Prompts\n\nUse these prompts during work to identify extraction opportunities:\n\n- \"What did I just learn that wasn't obvious before starting?\"\n- \"If I faced this exact problem again, what would I wish I knew?\"\n- \"What error message or symptom led me here, and what was the actual cause?\"\n- \"Is this pattern specific to this project, or would it help in similar projects?\"\n- \"What would I tell a colleague who hits this same issue?\"\n\n## Memory Consolidation\n\nWhen extracting skills, also consider:\n\n1. **Combining Related Knowledge**: If multiple related discoveries were made, consider \n whether they belong in one comprehensive skill or separate focused skills.\n\n2. **Updating Existing Skills**: Check if an existing skill should be updated rather than \n creating a new one.\n\n3. **Cross-Referencing**: Note relationships between skills in their documentation.\n\n## Quality Gates\n\nBefore finalizing a skill, verify:\n\n- [ ] Description contains specific trigger conditions\n- [ ] Solution has been verified to work\n- [ ] Content is specific enough to be actionable\n- [ ] Content is general enough to be reusable\n- [ ] No sensitive information (credentials, internal URLs) is included\n- [ ] Skill doesn't duplicate existing documentation or skills\n- [ ] Web research conducted when appropriate (for technology-specific topics)\n- [ ] References section included if web sources were consulted\n- [ ] Current best practices (post-2025) incorporated when relevant\n\n## Anti-Patterns to Avoid\n\n- **Over-extraction**: Not every task deserves a skill. Mundane solutions don't need preservation.\n- **Vague descriptions**: \"Helps with React problems\" won't surface when needed.\n- **Unverified solutions**: Only extract what actually worked.\n- **Documentation duplication**: Don't recreate official docs; link to them and add what's missing.\n- **Stale knowledge**: Mark skills with versions and dates; knowledge can become outdated.\n\n## Skill Lifecycle\n\nSkills should evolve:\n\n1. **Creation**: Initial extraction with documented verification\n2. **Refinement**: Update based on additional use cases or edge cases discovered\n3. **Deprecation**: Mark as deprecated when underlying tools/patterns change\n4. **Archival**: Remove or archive skills that are no longer relevant\n\n## Example: Complete Extraction Flow\n\n**Scenario**: While debugging a Next.js app, you discover that &#96;getServerSideProps&#96; errors\naren't showing in the browser console because they're server-side, and the actual error is\nin the terminal.\n\n**Step 1 - Identify the Knowledge**:\n- Problem: Server-side errors don't appear in browser console\n- Non-obvious aspect: Expected behavior for server-side code in Next.js\n- Trigger: Generic error page with empty browser console\n\n**Step 2 - Research Best Practices**:\nSearch: \"Next.js getServerSideProps error handling best practices 2026\"\n- Found official docs on error handling\n- Discovered recommended patterns for try-catch in data fetching\n- Learned about error boundaries for server components\n\n**Step 3-5 - Structure and Save**:\n\n**Extraction**:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;markdown\n---\nname: nextjs-server-side-error-debugging\ndescription: |\n  Debug getServerSideProps and getStaticProps errors in Next.js. Use when: \n  (1) Page shows generic error but browser console is empty, (2) API routes \n  return 500 with no details, (3) Server-side code fails silently. Check \n  terminal/server logs instead of browser for actual error messages.\nauthor: Claude Code\nversion: 1.0.0\ndate: 2024-01-15\n---\n\n# Next.js Server-Side Error Debugging\n\n## Problem\nServer-side errors in Next.js don't appear in the browser console, making \ndebugging frustrating when you're looking in the wrong place.\n\n## Context / Trigger Conditions\n- Page displays \"Internal Server Error\" or custom error page\n- Browser console shows no errors\n- Using getServerSideProps, getStaticProps, or API routes\n- Error only occurs on navigation/refresh, not on client-side transitions\n\n## Solution\n1. Check the terminal where &#96;npm run dev&#96; is running—errors appear there\n2. For production, check server logs (Vercel dashboard, CloudWatch, etc.)\n3. Add try-catch with console.error in server-side functions for clarity\n4. Use Next.js error handling: return &#96;{ notFound: true }&#96; or &#96;{ redirect: {...} }&#96; \n   instead of throwing\n\n## Verification\nAfter checking terminal, you should see the actual stack trace with file \nand line numbers.\n\n## Notes\n- This applies to all server-side code in Next.js, not just data fetching\n- In development, Next.js sometimes shows a modal with partial error info\n- The &#96;next.config.js&#96; option &#96;reactStrictMode&#96; can cause double-execution\n  that makes debugging confusing\n\n## References\n- [Next.js Data Fetching: getServerSideProps](https://nextjs.org/docs/pages/building-your-application/data-fetching/get-server-side-props)\n- [Next.js Error Handling](https://nextjs.org/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/error-handling)\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Integration with Workflow\n\n### Automatic Trigger Conditions\n\nInvoke this skill immediately after completing a task when ANY of these apply:\n\n1. **Non-obvious debugging**: The solution required \u003e10 minutes of investigation and\n wasn't found in documentation\n2. **Error resolution**: Fixed an error where the error message was misleading or the\n root cause wasn't obvious\n3. **Workaround discovery**: Found a workaround for a tool/framework limitation that\n required experimentation\n4. **Configuration insight**: Discovered project-specific setup that differs from\n standard patterns\n5. **Trial-and-error success**: Tried multiple approaches before finding what worked\n\n### Explicit Invocation\n\nAlso invoke when:\n- User runs &#96;/claudeception&#96; to review the session\n- User says \"save this as a skill\" or similar\n- User asks \"what did we learn?\"\n\n### Self-Check After Each Task\n\nAfter completing any significant task, ask yourself:\n- \"Did I just spend meaningful time investigating something?\"\n- \"Would future-me benefit from having this documented?\"\n- \"Was the solution non-obvious from documentation alone?\"\n\nIf yes to any, invoke this skill immediately.\n\nRemember: The goal is continuous, autonomous improvement. Every valuable discovery\nshould have the opportunity to benefit future work sessions.\n",
  "url": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/blader/Claudeception/main/SKILL.md"
}

_**Agent (claude-4.6-opus-high)**_

好，我已经完整调研了 Claudeception。现在做一个结构化评估。

---

## 调研结果：Claudeception

### 它是什么

Claudeception 是一个 **Claude Code 的自主学习系统**——当 AI 在工作过程中发现了非显而易见的调试技巧、变通方案、或项目特定模式时，自动把这些知识提取为可复用的 Skill 文件。下次遇到类似问题，Skill 自动加载，不用重新摸索。

核心理念来自学术研究（Voyager、CASCADE、Reflexion）：**能持久化学习成果的 Agent，比每次从零开始的 Agent 表现更好。**

### 工作机制

1. **Hook 注入**：通过 `settings.json` 的 `hooks.UserPromptSubmit` 配置，每次用户提交 prompt 时注入一段提醒文本，强制 Claude 在完成任务后自评"是否有可提取的知识"
2. **质量门控**：不是什么都提取——必须满足：非显而易见、可复用、有具体触发条件、已验证有效
3. **Skill 输出**：提取的知识写成标准 SKILL.md（YAML frontmatter + 问题/触发条件/解决方案/验证步骤），存到 `.claude/skills/` 或 `~/.claude/skills/`
4. **自动匹配**：Claude Code 启动时加载所有 Skill 的 name+description（约 100 token/个），语义匹配当前上下文自动调用

### 与我们项目的对比分析

| 维度 | Claudeception | 我们的系统 |
|------|---------------|-----------|
| **目标** | AI 自主积累调试/技术经验 | 用户画像+认知+决策经验的结构化沉淀 |
| **知识类型** | 技术性（错误修复、配置技巧、工具用法） | ABC 三类（画像/知识/用户观点） |
| **提取触发** | 调试完成后自动评估 | 管线驱动（用户审阅→复盘大脑→数字真我审核） |
| **质量控制** | 单层自评（Claude 自己判断值不值得提取） | 三层（用户审阅→复盘大脑分析→数字真我终审） |
| **存储** | Skill 文件（Markdown） | 三层架构（MD+JSON→向量库→有价值全文） |
| **消费方式** | Claude Code 语义匹配自动加载 | 各窗口读知识库 |
| **平台** | Claude Code（CLI） | Cursor IDE |

### 可借鉴的点

1. **"每次任务后自评"的 Hook 机制**——我们的工程窗口经常解决技术问题后知识就丢了（交接备忘里的"教训"就是手动版的这个），可以考虑在工程窗口加一个类似的自评环节
2. **Skill description 的语义匹配优化思路**——他们强调 description 要写得足够具体才能被匹配到，这对我们 SK 的 description 字段写法有参考价值
3. **质量门控清单**——"非显而易见 + 可复用 + 有触发条件 + 已验证"这四条标准，和我们的 ABC 入库标准逻辑一致，但更简洁

### 不适用的点

1. **平台不匹配**——Claudeception 是为 Claude Code（CLI 工具）设计的，用的是 `~/.claude/skills/` 路径和 Claude Code 的 Hook 系统。我们用的是 Cursor IDE，SK 体系是 `.cursor/skills/`，没有 `UserPromptSubmit` Hook 机制
2. **单层自评不够**——他们让 Claude 自己判断"值不值得提取"，我们的经验是 AI 自评有偏差风险（数字真我-11 明确说过"当事人不会分析自己为什么犯错"），所以我们用三层审核
3. **知识类型太窄**——只处理技术性知识，不处理画像/认知/决策模式，和我们的 ABC 体系目标不同

---

## 碎片整合

你的意思是：**让我去调研 Claudeception 这个工具，评估它的机制能不能用到我们的数字分身项目里**（不是直接安装它，而是看理念和机制有没有可借鉴的）。

🔵 事实：Claudeception 是 Claude Code 的自主学习 Skill，2000+ 星，机制是 Hook 注入 + 自评 + 自动提取 Skill
🟡 推断：你可能看到了这个工具觉得"自动学习"的理念和我们的知识沉淀目标有共鸣
🔴 待确认：你想要的是"借鉴理念改进我们的流程"，还是"直接移植某个具体功能"，还是其他

**理解对吗？**

[smixs/agent-memory-skill](https://github.com/smixs/agent-memory-skill)\n\n# GitHub AI Agent Skill Library, Persistent Memory \u0026 Knowledge Base (2025-2026)\n\n## Agent Skills Standard\n\nAn open standard for AI agent capabilities was announced by Anthropic on December 18, 2025.[1] Agent Skills is a simple format for packaging expertise and workflows into portable directories compatible with 16+ major AI tools including Claude Code, Cursor, OpenAI Codex, Gemini CLI, VS Code, GitHub Copilot, and others.[1] The standard uses a &#96;SKILL.md&#96; markdown file and supports progressive disclosure, allowing dozens or hundreds of skills to load without performance penalties.[1] It operates on a \"Write Once, Works Everywhere\" principle, making skills portable across all compatible platforms.[1]\n\n## Persistent Memory Solutions\n\n**Google's Always On Memory Agent**: Google open-sourced an \"Always On Memory Agent\" on GitHub under MIT License, built with the Agent Development Kit (ADK) and Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite.[2] The system runs continuously, ingests files and API input, stores structured memories in SQLite with scheduled consolidation every 30 minutes, and supports text, image, audio, video and PDF ingestion.[2] It notably avoids vector databases and embeddings, instead relying on LLMs to organize and update memory directly.[2]\n\n**Acontext**: An open-source skill memory layer for AI agents that automatically captures learnings from agent runs and stores them as agent skill files.[3] It uses Markdown files for memory storage (no embeddings or API lock-in), supports progressive disclosure retrieval via tool use, and allows exporting skills as ZIP for reuse across frameworks.[3] The project has 3,178 stars on GitHub with Apache 2.0 licensing.[3]\n\n**Agent Memory Skill**: A Python-based memory system using the Ebbinghaus forgetting curve for AI agents, featuring three-layer memory architecture (hot context, searchable vault, archive) with time-decay relevance scoring.[5]",

{
  "markdown": "\n \n \n \n \n 🌐 Website \n |\n 📚 Document \n \n \n \n \n \n \n \n \n \n \n \n \n \n\n\n\n\n\n\n## What is Acontext?\n\nAcontext is an open-source skill memory layer for AI agents. It **automatically** captures learnings from agent runs and stores them as **agent skill files** — files you can read, edit, and share across agents, LLMs, and frameworks.\n\nIf you want the agent you build to **learn from its mistakes** and **reuse what worked** — without opaque memory polluting your context — give Acontext a try.\n\n\n\n## Skill is All You Need\n\nAgent memory is getting increasingly complicated🤢 — hard to understand, hard to debug, and hard for users to inspect or correct. Acontext takes a different approach: if agent skills can represent every piece of knowledge an agent needs as simple files, so can the memory.\n\n- **Acontext builds memory in the agent skills format**, so everyone can see and understand what the memory actually contains.\n- **Skill is Memory, Memory is Skill**. Whether a skill comes from one you downloaded from Clawhub or one you created yourself, Acontext can follow it and evolve it over time.\n\n\n\n## The Philosophy of Acontext\n\n- **Plain file, any framework** — Skill memories are Markdown files. Use them with LangGraph, Claude, AI SDK, or anything that reads files. No embeddings, no API lock-in. Git, grep, and mount to the sandbox.\n- **You design the structure** — Attach more skills to define the schema, naming, and file layout of the memory. For example: one file per contact, one per project by uploading a working context skill.\n- **Progressive disclosure, not search** — The agent can use &#96;get_skill&#96; and &#96;get_skill_file&#96; to fetch what it needs. Retrieval is by tool use and reasoning, not semantic top-k.\n- **Download as ZIP, reuse anywhere** — Export skill files as ZIP. Run locally, in another agent, or with another LLM. No vendor lock-in; no re-embedding or migration step.\n\n## How It Works\n\n### Store — How skills get memorized?\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;mermaid\nflowchart LR\n  A[Session messages] --\u003e C[Task complete/failed]\n  C --\u003e D[Distillation]\n  D --\u003e E[Skill Agent]\n  E --\u003e F[Update Skills]\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n- **Session messages** — Conversation (and optionally tool calls, artifacts) is the raw input. Tasks are extracted from the message stream automatically (or inferred from explicit outcome reporting).\n- **Task complete or failed** — When a task is marked done or failed (e.g. by agent report or automatic detection), that outcome is the trigger for learning.\n- **Distillation** — An LLM pass infers from the conversation and execution trace what worked, what failed, and user preferences.\n- **Skill Agent** — Decides where to store (existing skill or new) and writes according to your &#96;SKILL.md&#96; schema.\n- **Update Skills** — Skills are updated. You define the structure in &#96;SKILL.md&#96;; the system does extraction, routing, and writing.\n\n### Recall — How the agent uses skills on the next run\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;mermaid\nflowchart LR\n  E[Any Agent] --\u003e F[list_skills/get_skill]\n  F --\u003e G[Appear in context]\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nGive your agent **Skill Content Tools** (&#96;get_skill&#96;, &#96;get_skill_file&#96;). The agent decides what it needs, calls the tools, and gets the skill content. No embedding search — **progressive disclosure, agent in the loop**.\n\n\n\n# 🪜 Use It to Improve your Agent\n\nClaude Code: \n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;text\nRead https://acontext.io/SKILL.md and follow the instructions to install and configure Acontext for Claude Code\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nOpenClaw:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;text\nRead https://acontext.io/SKILL.md and follow the instructions to install and configure Acontext for OpenClaw\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n\n\n\n# 🚀 Step-by-step Quickstart\n\n### Connect to Acontext\n\n1. Go to [Acontext.io](https://acontext.io), claim your free credits.\n2. Go through a one-click onboarding to get your API Key (starts with &#96;sk-ac&#96;)\n\n \n \n \n \n \n\n\n\n\n \n 💻 Self-host Acontext \n\nWe have an &#96;acontext-cli&#96; to help you do a quick proof-of-concept. Download it first in your terminal:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\ncurl -fsSL https://install.acontext.io | sh\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nYou should have [docker](https://www.docker.com/get-started/) installed and an OpenAI API Key to start an Acontext backend on your computer:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\nmkdir acontext_server \u0026\u0026 cd acontext_server\nacontext server up\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n\u003e Make sure your LLM has the ability to [call tools](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/function-calling). By default, Acontext will use &#96;gpt-4.1&#96;.\n\n&#96;acontext server up&#96; will create/use &#96;.env&#96; and &#96;config.yaml&#96; for Acontext, and create a &#96;db&#96; folder to persist data.\n\n\n\nOnce it's done, you can access the following endpoints:\n\n- Acontext API Base URL: http://localhost:8029/api/v1\n- Acontext Dashboard: http://localhost:3000/\n\n \n\n\n\n### Install SDKs\n\nWe're maintaining Python [![pypi](https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/acontext.svg)](https://pypi.org/project/acontext/) and Typescript [![npm](https://img.shields.io/npm/v/@acontext/acontext.svg?logo=npm\u0026logoColor=fff\u0026style=flat\u0026labelColor=2C2C2C\u0026color=28CF8D)](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@acontext/acontext) SDKs. The snippets below are using Python.\n\n\u003e Click the doc link to see TS SDK Quickstart.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\npip install acontext\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n\n### Initialize Client\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;python\nimport os\nfrom acontext import AcontextClient\n\n# For cloud:\nclient = AcontextClient(\n    api_key=os.getenv(\"ACONTEXT_API_KEY\"),\n)\n\n# For self-hosted:\nclient = AcontextClient(\n    base_url=\"http://localhost:8029/api/v1\",\n    api_key=\"sk-ac-your-root-api-bearer-token\",\n)\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n\n\n### Skill Memory in Action\n\nCreate a learning space, attach a session, and let the agent learn — skills are written as Markdown files automatically.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;python\nfrom acontext import AcontextClient\n\nclient = AcontextClient(api_key=\"sk-ac-...\")\n\n# Create a learning space and attach a session\nspace = client.learning_spaces.create()\nsession = client.sessions.create()\nclient.learning_spaces.learn(space.id, session_id=session.id)\n\n# Run your agent, store messages — when tasks complete, learning runs automatically\nclient.sessions.store_message(session.id, blob={\"role\": \"user\", \"content\": \"My name is Gus\"})\nclient.sessions.store_message(session.id, blob={\"role\": \"assistant\", \"content\": \"Hi Gus! How can I help you today?\"})\n# ... agent runs ...\n\n# List learned skills (Markdown files)\nclient.learning_spaces.wait_for_learning(space.id, session_id=session.id)\nskills = client.learning_spaces.list_skills(space.id)\n\n# Download all skill files to a local directory\nfor skill in skills:\n    client.skills.download(skill_id=skill.id, path=f\"./skills/{skill.name}\")\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n\u003e &#96;wait_for_learning&#96; is a blocking helper for demo purposes. In production, task extraction and learning run in the background automatically — your agent never waits.\n\n### More Features\n\n- **[Context Engineering](https://docs.acontext.io/engineering/editing)** — Compress context with summaries and edit strategies\n- **[Disk](https://docs.acontext.io/store/disk)** — Virtual, persistent filesystem for agents\n- **[Sandbox](https://docs.acontext.io/store/sandbox)** — Isolated code execution with bash, Python, and [mountable skills](https://docs.acontext.io/tool/bash_tools#mounting-skills-in-sandbox)\n- **[Agent Tools](https://docs.acontext.io/tool/whatis)** — Disk tools, sandbox tools, and skill tools for LLM function calling\n\n\n\n\n\n# 🧐 Use Acontext to Build Agents\n\nDownload end-to-end scripts with &#96;acontext&#96;:\n\n**Python**\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\nacontext create my-proj --template-path \"python/openai-basic\"\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nMore examples on Python:\n\n- &#96;python/openai-agent-basic&#96;: openai agent sdk template\n- &#96;python/openai-agent-artifacts&#96;: agent can edit and download artifacts\n- &#96;python/claude-agent-sdk&#96;: claude agent sdk with &#96;ClaudeAgentStorage&#96;\n- &#96;python/agno-basic&#96;: agno framework template\n- &#96;python/smolagents-basic&#96;: smolagents (huggingface) template\n- &#96;python/interactive-agent-skill&#96;: interactive sandbox with mountable agent skills\n\n**Typescript**\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\nacontext create my-proj --template-path \"typescript/openai-basic\"\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nMore examples on Typescript:\n- &#96;typescript/vercel-ai-basic&#96;: agent in @vercel/ai-sdk\n- &#96;typescript/claude-agent-sdk&#96;: claude agent sdk with &#96;ClaudeAgentStorage&#96;\n- &#96;typescript/interactive-agent-skill&#96;: interactive sandbox with mountable agent skills\n\n\n\n\u003e [!NOTE]\n\u003e\n\u003e Check our example repo for more templates: [Acontext-Examples](https://github.com/memodb-io/Acontext-Examples).\n\u003e\n\u003e We're cooking more full-stack Agent Applications! {
  "markdown": "# Claude Code Semantic Memory System\n\nA persistent memory system for Claude Code that extracts learnings from past sessions and injects relevant context on every prompt.\n\n## The Problem\n\nClaude Code sessions are stateless by default. Every time context compacts or you start a new session, Claude forgets:\n- Solutions you already discovered together\n- Gotchas and traps you identified\n- Your infrastructure details and preferences\n- Decisions you made and why\n\nThis leads to repeated mistakes, redundant conversations, and lost productivity.\n\n## The Solution\n\nThis system gives Claude **persistent memory** across sessions:\n\n1. **Convert** your &#96;.jsonl&#96; transcripts to readable markdown\n2. **Extract** learnings using Claude sub-agents that process transcripts\n3. **Embed** learnings with a local embedding model (nomic-embed-text)\n4. **Inject** relevant memories via Claude Code hooks that fire on every prompt\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐\n│  Your Prompt    │────►│  Hook Fires     │────►│  Query Daemon   │\n│                 │     │  (mechanical)   │     │  (cosine sim)   │\n└─────────────────┘     └─────────────────┘     └────────┬────────┘\n                                                         │\n                                                         ▼\n┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐\n│  Claude sees    │◄────│  Inject as XML  │◄────│  Top 3 memories │\n│  context + mem  │     │  in context     │     │  (≥0.45 sim)    │\n└─────────────────┘     └─────────────────┘     └─────────────────┘\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n---\n\n## Quick Start\n\n### Prerequisites\n\n- [Node.js](https://nodejs.org/) (for transcript conversion)\n- [Ollama](https://ollama.com/) (for local embeddings)\n- Python 3.8+ (for the memory daemon)\n- Claude Code CLI\n\n### 1. Install Dependencies\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n# Install Ollama\ncurl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh\n\n# Pull the embedding model\nollama pull nomic-embed-text\n\n# Clone this repo\ngit clone https://github.com/zacdcook/claude-code-semantic-memory.git\ncd claude-code-semantic-memory\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### 2. Convert Your Transcripts\n\nClaude Code stores session transcripts as &#96;.jsonl&#96; files in &#96;~/.claude/projects/&#96;. Convert them to readable markdown:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\nnode scripts/jsonl-to-markdown.js ~/.claude/projects/ ./converted-transcripts/\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThis extracts user messages, assistant messages (including thinking blocks), and system prompts. Tool calls and results are stripped for cleaner extraction.\n\n### 3. Extract Learnings\n\nStart a new Claude Code session and use the extraction prompt:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\nclaude\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThen paste the contents of &#96;prompts/extract-learnings.md&#96;. Claude will:\n\n1. List all &#96;.md&#96; files in your converted transcripts folder\n2. Dispatch sub-agents in parallel to process batches\n3. Each sub-agent extracts structured learnings\n4. Store learnings via the daemon's &#96;/store&#96; endpoint\n5. Output to &#96;~/extracted-learnings.jsonl&#96;\n\n### 4. Start the Memory Daemon\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\ncd daemon\npip install -r requirements.txt\npython server.py\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe daemon runs on port 8741 and provides:\n- &#96;POST /store&#96; - Embed and store a learning\n- &#96;POST /recall&#96; - Query for relevant memories\n- &#96;GET /health&#96; - Health check\n\n### 5. Import Your Learnings\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\npython scripts/import-learnings.py ~/extracted-learnings.jsonl\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### 6. Install the Hooks\n\nCopy all hooks to your Claude Code hooks directory:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n# Session initialization\ncp hooks/session-start.sh ~/.claude/hooks/SessionStart.sh\n\n# Memory injection on prompts\ncp hooks/user-prompt-submit.sh ~/.claude/hooks/UserPromptSubmit.sh\n\n# Memory injection during iteration\ncp hooks/pre-tool-use.sh ~/.claude/hooks/PreToolUse.sh\n\n# Auto-export on compaction\ncp hooks/pre-compact.sh ~/.claude/hooks/PreCompact.sh\n\n# Make executable\nchmod +x ~/.claude/hooks/*.sh\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nNow:\n- Every prompt automatically queries memory and injects relevant learnings\n- During iteration, Claude's thinking is analyzed for additional relevant memories\n- When context compacts, the transcript is exported and a sub-agent extracts learnings\n\n---\n\n## Architecture\n\n### Hook Lifecycle\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nSESSION START\n════════════\n┌─────────────────┐\n│  SessionStart   │ → Check daemon health\n│                 │ → Warn about orphaned transcripts\n└────────┬────────┘\n         │\nACTIVE WORK (repeats for each user message)\n════════════\n         ▼\n┌─────────────────┐\n│UserPromptSubmit │ → Embed user's prompt\n│                 │ → Query daemon /recall\n│                 │ → Inject top 3 memories\n└────────┬────────┘\n         │\n         ▼  (fires before EACH tool)\n┌─────────────────┐\n│  PreToolUse     │ → Extract Claude's thinking\n│                 │ → Query for new relevant memories\n│                 │ → Inject if thinking has drifted\n└────────┬────────┘\n         │\nCONTEXT COMPACTION (when context window fills)\n════════════════════\n         ▼\n┌─────────────────┐\n│  PreCompact     │ → Export transcript to disk\n│                 │ → Convert JSONL to markdown\n│                 │ → Output sub-agent dispatch instructions\n└────────┬────────┘\n         │\n         ▼\n┌─────────────────┐\n│  Sub-Agent      │ → Read exported transcript\n│  (Task tool)    │ → Extract learnings\n│                 │ → Store via daemon /store endpoint\n└─────────────────┘\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Sub-Agent Extraction on Compaction\n\nWhen context compacts, the PreCompact hook exports the transcript and outputs instructions for Claude to dispatch a sub-agent. The sub-agent:\n\n1. Reads the exported markdown transcript\n2. Extracts learnings (solutions, gotchas, patterns, etc.)\n3. Stores each learning via the daemon's &#96;/store&#96; endpoint\n\nThis keeps everything within Claude Code - no external API calls needed. The sub-agent uses the Task tool to run without blocking.\n\n### Learning Types\n\n| Type | Description | Example |\n|------|-------------|---------|\n| &#96;WORKING_SOLUTION&#96; | Confirmed working commands/patterns | \"Use &#96;Import-Clixml&#96; for PowerShell credentials over Tailscale\" |\n| &#96;GOTCHA&#96; | Traps and counterintuitive behaviors | \"Git Bash strips &#96;$&#96; variables before PowerShell sees them\" |\n| &#96;PATTERN&#96; | Recurring architectural decisions | \"Check HOSTNAME in hooks to determine daemon URL\" |\n| &#96;DECISION&#96; | Explicit design choices with reasoning | \"Using nomic-embed-text for 8K token context\" |\n| &#96;FAILURE&#96; | What didn't work and why | \"SSH-based PS remoting fails due to TTY requirements\" |\n| &#96;PREFERENCE&#96; | User's stated preferences | \"Query memory before asking clarifying questions\" |\n\n### Why These Design Choices\n\n| Decision | Why |\n|----------|-----|\n| **Hooks over CLAUDE.md** | Hooks fire deterministically; CLAUDE.md instructions are suggestions Claude may skip under cognitive load |\n| **nomic-embed-text over MiniLM** | 8K token context vs 256 tokens — MiniLM truncates 75% of longer conversation turns |\n| **0.45 similarity threshold** | Permissive enough to catch semantically related content, not so low it floods with noise |\n| **Sub-agent extraction** | Uses Claude Code's own capabilities; no external API keys or local LLMs needed |\n\n### Database Schema\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;sql\nCREATE TABLE learnings (\n    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,\n    type TEXT NOT NULL,\n    content TEXT NOT NULL,\n    context TEXT,\n    embedding BLOB NOT NULL,\n    confidence REAL DEFAULT 0.9,\n    session_source TEXT,\n    created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP\n);\n\nCREATE INDEX idx_learnings_type ON learnings(type);\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n---\n\n## File Structure\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nclaude-code-semantic-memory/\n├── README.md\n├── scripts/\n│   ├── jsonl-to-markdown.js    # Convert transcripts\n│   └── import-learnings.py     # Import JSONL to database\n├── prompts/\n│   └── extract-learnings.md    # Prompt for sub-agent extraction\n├── daemon/\n│   ├── server.py               # Flask API server\n│   ├── requirements.txt\n│   └── config.json             # Similarity thresholds, model config\n├── hooks/\n│   ├── session-start.sh        # Check daemon, warn orphans\n│   ├── user-prompt-submit.sh   # Memory injection on prompts\n│   ├── pre-tool-use.sh         # Memory injection during iteration\n│   └── pre-compact.sh          # Auto-export and dispatch sub-agent\n└── examples/\n    └── sample-learnings.jsonl  # Example output format\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n---\n\n## Configuration\n\nEdit &#96;daemon/config.json&#96;:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;json\n{\n  \"embeddingModel\": \"nomic-embed-text\",\n  \"minSimilarity\": 0.45,\n  \"maxResults\": 3,\n  \"duplicateThreshold\": 0.92,\n  \"timeoutMs\": 2500,\n  \"port\": 8741\n}\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n| Parameter | Default | Description |\n|-----------|---------|-------------|\n| &#96;embeddingModel&#96; | &#96;nomic-embed-text&#96; | Ollama model for embeddings (768 dimensions) |\n| &#96;minSimilarity&#96; | &#96;0.45&#96; | Minimum cosine similarity to return a memory |\n| &#96;maxResults&#96; | &#96;3&#96; | Maximum memories to inject per query |\n| &#96;duplicateThreshold&#96; | &#96;0.92&#96; | Similarity threshold for deduplication |\n| &#96;timeoutMs&#96; | &#96;2500&#96; | Max time to wait for embedding |\n| &#96;port&#96; | &#96;8741&#96; | Daemon port |\n\n---\n\n## Advanced Usage\n\n### Multi-Machine Setup\n\nIf you run Claude Code on a laptop but want embeddings on a GPU desktop:\n\n1. Run the daemon on your desktop\n2. Connect both machines via Tailscale (or any VPN)\n3. Set &#96;CLAUDE_DAEMON_HOST&#96; environment variable on your laptop:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\nexport CLAUDE_DAEMON_HOST=100.95.72.101  # Desktop's Tailscale IP\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe hook will query the remote daemon instead of localhost.\n\n---\n\n## Troubleshooting\n\n### Hook not firing\n- Check hook is executable: &#96;chmod +x ~/.claude/hooks/*.sh&#96;\n- Verify daemon is running: &#96;curl http://localhost:8741/health&#96;\n\n### No memories returned\n- Check similarity threshold isn't too high\n- Verify learnings were imported: &#96;curl http://localhost:8741/stats&#96;\n- Try a more specific query\n\n### Slow embedding\n- Ensure Ollama is using GPU: &#96;ollama ps&#96; should show CUDA\n- Reduce batch sizes if running out of memory\n\n---\n\n## Credits\n\n- [Ollama](https://ollama.com/) — Local embedding infrastructure\n- [nomic-embed-text](https://huggingface.co/nomic-ai/nomic-embed-text-v1.5) — Embedding model with 8K context\n\n---\n\n## License\n\nMIT\n",
  "url": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/zacdcook/claude-code-semantic-memory/main/README.md"
}

{
  "markdown": "# agent-memory-skill\n\n[![License: MIT](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-MIT-blue.svg)](LICENSE)\n[![Python 3.10+](https://img.shields.io/badge/Python-3.10+-green.svg)](https://python.org)\n[![No Dependencies](https://img.shields.io/badge/Dependencies-Zero-brightgreen.svg)](#)\n\n**Memory system for AI agents based on the Ebbinghaus forgetting curve.**\n\nOne Python file. Zero dependencies. Works with any directory of markdown files.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n  Memory Strength\n  1.0 ┤████████████████████████████████████████████████  ← just accessed\n      │ ╲\n  0.9 ┤   ╲                                              ← Day 7: active\n      │     ╲\n  0.8 ┤       ╲\n      │         ╲\n  0.7 ┤           ╲                                       ← Day 21: warm\n      │             ╲\n  0.6 ┤               ╲\n      │                 ╲\n  0.5 ┤                   ╲                               ← Day 33: cold\n      │                     ╲\n  0.4 ┤                       ╲\n      │                         ╲\n  0.3 ┤                           ╲\n      │                             ╲\n  0.2 ┤                               ╲\n      │                                 ╲\n  0.1 ┤─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─floor  ← Day 60+: archive\n      │\n  0.0 ┤\n      └──┬──────┬──────────────┬────────────┬──────────→  Days\n         0      7              21           60\n\n         active    warm          cold         archive\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n---\n\n## The Problem\n\nAI agents forget everything between sessions. Two common solutions — and why they don't work:\n\n**\"Dump everything into context\"** — Loads entire knowledge bases per turn. Burns 20K+ tokens before the conversation starts. No prioritization: a 2-month-old contact gets the same weight as yesterday's hot lead.\n\n**\"Vector search everything\"** — Retrieves by semantic similarity but has no temporal awareness. A card accessed yesterday and a card untouched for 90 days look identical. No concept of \"fading\" or \"forgetting.\" No serendipity.\n\nHuman memory does something different: it **decays over time**, **strengthens with use**, and occasionally **surprises you** with random connections. This skill brings that to AI agents.\n\n---\n\n## The Solution — 3-Layer Memory\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n│  Layer 1: HOT CONTEXT              always loaded, \u003c4KB  │\n│  └── State file — active focus, blockers, reminders     │\n├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤\n│  Layer 2: SEARCHABLE VAULT          on-demand, unlimited │\n│  └── Cards with YAML frontmatter — one file per entity  │\n│      Tiers: core → active → warm → cold → archive       │\n├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤\n│  Layer 3: ARCHIVE                   deep/creative only   │\n│  └── Old logs, completed projects, cold contacts         │\n│      Still searchable, excluded from default queries     │\n└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n**Rule:** Each fact lives in ONE place. If it's in a card, don't duplicate it in the state file.\n\nSee [docs/architecture.md](docs/architecture.md) for full design rationale.\n\n---\n\n## Forgetting Curve\n\nInspired by Hermann Ebbinghaus (1885). Each card has a &#96;relevance&#96; score (0.0–1.0) that decays linearly:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nrelevance = max(0.1, 1.0 - days × 0.015)\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Tier Assignment\n\n| Tier | Days Since Access | Relevance | Behavior |\n|------|-------------------|-----------|----------|\n| **core** | manual | 1.0 | Never auto-demoted. Identity, security, pricing. |\n| **active** | 0–7 | 1.0–0.90 | Searched in all modes. Hot context. |\n| **warm** | 8–21 | 0.89–0.69 | Default search. Gradually fading. |\n| **cold** | 22–60 | 0.68–0.10 | Deep search only. Mostly forgotten. |\n| **archive** | 60+ | 0.10 (floor) | Creative mode or explicit recall. |\n\n### Graduated Touch (Spaced Repetition)\n\nUnlike a simple \"reset to top,\" &#96;touch&#96; promotes **one tier at a time**:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\narchive → cold → warm → active → active (refresh)\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nMultiple reads = stronger memory. Natural spaced repetition without manual scheduling.\n\n---\n\n## Search Modes\n\n| Mode | Tiers Searched | When to Use | Token Cost |\n|------|---------------|-------------|------------|\n| **heartbeat** | core + active | Quick status checks, monitoring | ~2K |\n| **normal** | active + warm | Most questions, task execution | ~5K |\n| **deep** | all tiers | Strategy, \"find everything about X\" | ~15K |\n| **creative** | random cold+archive | Brainstorming, ideation, \"what if\" | ~3K |\n\n### Creative Mode — The Highlight Feature\n\nHuman creativity comes from random associations — shower thoughts, serendipitous encounters, dreams. Creative mode simulates this by pulling **random forgotten cards** back into working memory.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\npython3 memory-engine.py creative 5 vault/\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n  creative recall — 5 random cards:\n    [cold] Cloud Migration Strategy\n           projects/cloud-migration.md (r=0.25, last=2026-01-05)\n    [archive] React Native Performance Notes\n           notes/rn-performance.md (r=0.1, last=2025-11-20)\n    ...\n\n  read these cards and look for unexpected connections to your current task\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe randomness is the feature, not a bug.\n\nSee [docs/search-protocols.md](docs/search-protocols.md) for detailed protocols.\n\n---\n\n## Quick Start\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n# 1. Copy to your project\ncurl -O https://raw.githubusercontent.com/smixs/agent-memory-skill/main/memory-engine.py\n\n# 2. Scan your vault\npython3 memory-engine.py scan vault/\n\n# 3. Initialize cards (add YAML frontmatter)\npython3 memory-engine.py init vault/ --dry-run   # preview first\npython3 memory-engine.py init vault/              # apply\n\n# 4. Run decay (update relevance scores)\npython3 memory-engine.py decay vault/\n\n# 5. Creative recall (surface forgotten cards)\npython3 memory-engine.py creative 5 vault/\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n---\n\n## All Commands\n\n| Command | Description | Example |\n|---------|-------------|---------|\n| &#96;scan&#96; | Analyze files, report stats (no changes) | &#96;memory-engine.py scan vault/&#96; |\n| &#96;init&#96; | Add YAML frontmatter to files missing it | &#96;memory-engine.py init vault/ --dry-run&#96; |\n| &#96;decay&#96; | Update relevance scores and tiers | &#96;memory-engine.py decay vault/&#96; |\n| &#96;touch&#96; | Promote card one tier up (graduated recall) | &#96;memory-engine.py touch vault/crm/acme.md&#96; |\n| &#96;creative&#96; | Random N cards from cold/archive tiers | &#96;memory-engine.py creative 5 vault/&#96; |\n| &#96;daily&#96; | Bootstrap and decay daily files (YYYY-MM-DD.md) | &#96;memory-engine.py daily vault/daily/&#96; |\n| &#96;stats&#96; | Show tier distribution and health metrics | &#96;memory-engine.py stats vault/&#96; |\n| &#96;config&#96; | Generate default &#96;.memory-config.json&#96; | &#96;memory-engine.py config vault/&#96; |\n\n### Global Options\n\n| Flag | Description |\n|------|-------------|\n| &#96;--dry-run&#96; | Preview changes without writing files |\n| &#96;--verbose&#96; | Show per-file details |\n| &#96;--config &#96; | Custom config file path |\n\n---\n\n## YAML Schema\n\n### Required Fields\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\n---\ntype: crm                         # crm | lead | contact | project | personal | daily | note\ndescription: \u003e-                   # One-line search snippet\n  Cloud provider, enterprise tier\ntags: [cloud, enterprise]          # 2-5 freeform tags\nstatus: active                    # active | draft | pending | done | inactive\n---\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Auto-Managed Fields (by memory-engine.py)\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\nlast_accessed: 2026-02-20         # When card was last read/touched\nrelevance: 0.85                   # 0.0-1.0, decays over time\ntier: active                      # core | active | warm | cold | archive\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Full Example\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\n---\ntype: crm\ndescription: \u003e-\n  Cloud infrastructure provider, enterprise tier, renewal Q2 2026\ntags: [cloud, enterprise, renewal]\nstatus: active\nindustry: IT\nregion: US\ncreated: 2026-01-15\nupdated: 2026-02-20\nlast_accessed: 2026-02-20\nrelevance: 0.85\ntier: active\n---\n\n# Acme Cloud Corp\n\n## Overview\n- **Industry:** Cloud Infrastructure\n- **Contact:** Jane Smith, VP Sales\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nSee [docs/yaml-schema.md](docs/yaml-schema.md) for the full schema reference.\n\n---\n\n## Configuration\n\nGenerate a default config:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\npython3 memory-engine.py config vault/\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nCreates &#96;.memory-config.json&#96;:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;json\n{\n  \"tiers\": {\"active\": 7, \"warm\": 21, \"cold\": 60},\n  \"decay_rate\": 0.015,\n  \"relevance_floor\": 0.1,\n  \"skip_patterns\": [\"_index.md\"],\n  \"type_inference\": {\"crm/\": \"crm\", \"leads/\": \"lead\"},\n  \"use_git_dates\": true\n}\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Domain-Specific Tuning\n\n| Domain | Active | Warm | Cold | Rate | Why |\n|--------|--------|------|------|------|-----|\n| Sales/CRM | 3 | 10 | 30 | 0.025 | Fast-moving, deals expire quickly |\n| Default | 7 | 21 | 60 | 0.015 | Balanced for general use |\n| Research | 14 | 45 | 120 | 0.008 | Knowledge stays relevant longer |\n\n### Config Fields\n\n| Field | Type | Default | Description |\n|-------|------|---------|-------------|\n| &#96;tiers.active&#96; | int | 7 | Days threshold for active tier |\n| &#96;tiers.warm&#96; | int | 21 | Days threshold for warm tier |\n| &#96;tiers.cold&#96; | int | 60 | Days threshold for cold tier |\n| &#96;decay_rate&#96; | float | 0.015 | Relevance loss per day |\n| &#96;relevance_floor&#96; | float | 0.1 | Minimum relevance (never reaches 0) |\n| &#96;skip_patterns&#96; | list | &#96;[\"_index.md\"]&#96; | Glob patterns to skip |\n| &#96;type_inference&#96; | object | &#96;{}&#96; | Path → type mapping for auto-inference |\n| &#96;use_git_dates&#96; | bool | true | Use git log for date resolution |\n\n---\n\n## Integration with Claude Code\n\n### As a Skill\n\nCopy the files into your project's skill directory:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n.claude/skills/agent-memory/\n├── SKILL.md              # Skill definition (Claude reads this)\n├── scripts/\n│   └── memory-engine.py  # Engine\n└── references/\n    ├── architecture.md\n    ├── search-protocols.md\n    └── yaml-schema.md\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nClaude Code will automatically discover and use the skill based on &#96;SKILL.md&#96;.\n\n### Daily Decay via Cron\n\nAdd to your crontab for automatic forgetting:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n# Run decay every day at midnight\n0 0 * * * cd /path/to/vault \u0026\u0026 python3 memory-engine.py decay .\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nOr via systemd timer for more reliability.\n\n### Full System\n\nFor the complete voice-first AI assistant (Telegram → Obsidian + Todoist), see [agent-second-brain](https://github.com/smixs/agent-second-brain).\n\n---\n\n## How It Compares\n\n| | Dump All | Vector DB | **agent-memory** |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| **Context cost** | High (all cards every turn) | Medium (top-K results) | Low (tiered loading) |\n| **Temporal awareness** | None | None | Built-in (decay + tiers) |\n| **Serendipity** | None | None | Creative mode |\n| **Dependencies** | None | Embedding model + DB | None (pure Python) |\n| **Setup** | Copy files | Deploy infra | Copy one file |\n| **Spaced repetition** | No | No | Graduated touch |\n| **Works offline** | Yes | Needs model API | Yes |\n\n---\n\n## Anti-Patterns\n\n| Don't | Do Instead |\n|-------|------------|\n| Load all cards into context | Search on demand, filter by tier |\n| Store same fact in 3 files | One card per entity, reference via links |\n| Delete old files to \"save space\" | Let tier decay handle visibility |\n| Touch every card during bulk ops | Only touch on meaningful read/update |\n| Build elaborate review systems | Let decay handle it — unused = fades |\n\n---\n\n## License\n\n[MIT](LICENSE)\n",

[[FEATURE] Add &#96;additionalContext&#96; support to PreToolUse hooks · Issue #15664 · anthropics/claude-code](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/15664)\n\n# Homunculus: Claude Code Plugin with Instinct-Based Learning\n\n**Homunculus** is a Claude Code plugin created by humanplane that observes your work patterns and evolves itself to help you better.[1] It's currently at v2.0-alpha as of January 2026.[1]\n\n## Core Functionality\n\nThe plugin uses **hooks for 100% reliable observation** and **instincts as the atomic unit of learned behavior**.[1] Key features include:\n\n- **Observes everything** — hooks capture every prompt and tool use[1]\n- **Learns instincts** — small behavioral rules with triggers, actions, and confidence scores[1]\n- **Evolves capabilities** — when instincts cluster, bigger structures emerge[1]\n\n## Architecture\n\nHomunculus uses a structured approach:[1]\n- **Hooks** — event-driven reflexes for observation\n- **Instincts** — atomic learned behaviors (trigger + action + confidence 0.3-0.9)[1]\n- **Skills** — auto-triggered behaviors (session-memory, instinct-apply)\n- **Commands** — user-invoked shortcuts (/init, /status, /evolve)\n- **Agents** — isolated specialists (background observer using Haiku model)\n\n## v1 vs v2 Changes\n\nV1 relied on probabilistic skills that fired only 50-80% of the time.[1] V2 improved this by using hooks for 100% reliable observation, running analysis in a background agent, and evolving instincts as the foundational unit before clustering into larger structures.[1]\n\n## Related: PreToolUse Hook Enhancement\n\nA separate Claude Code feature request (closed/completed January 2026) proposed adding &#96;additionalContext&#96; support to PreToolUse hooks to inject context at the exact moment tool use occurs.[5]",

{
  "markdown": "---\nname: continuous-learning\ndescription: Automatically extract reusable patterns from Claude Code sessions and save them as learned skills for future use.\norigin: ECC\n---\n\n# Continuous Learning Skill\n\nAutomatically evaluates Claude Code sessions on end to extract reusable patterns that can be saved as learned skills.\n\n## When to Activate\n\n- Setting up automatic pattern extraction from Claude Code sessions\n- Configuring the Stop hook for session evaluation\n- Reviewing or curating learned skills in &#96;~/.claude/skills/learned/&#96;\n- Adjusting extraction thresholds or pattern categories\n- Comparing v1 (this) vs v2 (instinct-based) approaches\n\n## How It Works\n\nThis skill runs as a **Stop hook** at the end of each session:\n\n1. **Session Evaluation**: Checks if session has enough messages (default: 10+)\n2. **Pattern Detection**: Identifies extractable patterns from the session\n3. **Skill Extraction**: Saves useful patterns to &#96;~/.claude/skills/learned/&#96;\n\n## Configuration\n\nEdit &#96;config.json&#96; to customize:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;json\n{\n  \"min_session_length\": 10,\n  \"extraction_threshold\": \"medium\",\n  \"auto_approve\": false,\n  \"learned_skills_path\": \"~/.claude/skills/learned/\",\n  \"patterns_to_detect\": [\n    \"error_resolution\",\n    \"user_corrections\",\n    \"workarounds\",\n    \"debugging_techniques\",\n    \"project_specific\"\n  ],\n  \"ignore_patterns\": [\n    \"simple_typos\",\n    \"one_time_fixes\",\n    \"external_api_issues\"\n  ]\n}\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Pattern Types\n\n| Pattern | Description |\n|---------|-------------|\n| &#96;error_resolution&#96; | How specific errors were resolved |\n| &#96;user_corrections&#96; | Patterns from user corrections |\n| &#96;workarounds&#96; | Solutions to framework/library quirks |\n| &#96;debugging_techniques&#96; | Effective debugging approaches |\n| &#96;project_specific&#96; | Project-specific conventions |\n\n## Hook Setup\n\nAdd to your &#96;~/.claude/settings.json&#96;:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;json\n{\n  \"hooks\": {\n    \"Stop\": [{\n      \"matcher\": \"*\",\n      \"hooks\": [{\n        \"type\": \"command\",\n        \"command\": \"~/.claude/skills/continuous-learning/evaluate-session.sh\"\n      }]\n    }]\n  }\n}\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Why Stop Hook?\n\n- **Lightweight**: Runs once at session end\n- **Non-blocking**: Doesn't add latency to every message\n- **Complete context**: Has access to full session transcript\n\n## Related\n\n- [The Longform Guide](https://x.com/affaanmustafa/status/2014040193557471352) - Section on continuous learning\n- &#96;/learn&#96; command - Manual pattern extraction mid-session\n\n---\n\n## Comparison Notes (Research: Jan 2025)\n\n### vs Homunculus\n\nHomunculus v2 takes a more sophisticated approach:\n\n| Feature | Our Approach | Homunculus v2 |\n|---------|--------------|---------------|\n| Observation | Stop hook (end of session) | PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks (100% reliable) |\n| Analysis | Main context | Background agent (Haiku) |\n| Granularity | Full skills | Atomic \"instincts\" |\n| Confidence | None | 0.3-0.9 weighted |\n| Evolution | Direct to skill | Instincts → cluster → skill/command/agent |\n| Sharing | None | Export/import instincts |\n\n**Key insight from homunculus:**\n\u003e \"v1 relied on skills to observe. Skills are probabilistic—they fire ~50-80% of the time. v2 uses hooks for observation (100% reliable) and instincts as the atomic unit of learned behavior.\"\n\n### Potential v2 Enhancements\n\n1. **Instinct-based learning** - Smaller, atomic behaviors with confidence scoring\n2. **Background observer** - Haiku agent analyzing in parallel\n3. **Confidence decay** - Instincts lose confidence if contradicted\n4. **Domain tagging** - code-style, testing, git, debugging, etc.\n5. **Evolution path** - Cluster related instincts into skills/commands\n\nSee: &#96;docs/continuous-learning-v2-spec.md&#96; for full spec.\n",

{
  "markdown": "# Homunculus\n\n![Homunculus](cover.png)\n\n\u003e **v2.0-alpha** — A complete rewrite. Instinct-based learning. Reliable observation. Real evolution.\n\n---\n\nIn old alchemical texts, a homunculus was a tiny being grown in a sealed vessel—alive, aware, bound to its creator alone.\n\nThis is that idea, alive in your terminal.\n\n---\n\n## Quick Start\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n# Add the marketplace\n/plugin marketplace add humanplane/homunculus\n\n# Install the plugin\n/plugin install homunculus@homunculus\n\n# Birth it\n/homunculus:init\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n---\n\n## What Is This?\n\nHomunculus is a **Claude Code plugin** that tries to be more than a tool. It:\n\n- **Observes everything** — hooks capture every prompt and tool use\n- **Learns instincts** — small behavioral rules with triggers and actions\n- **Evolves capabilities** — when instincts cluster, bigger structures emerge\n- **Adapts its personality** — based on your technical level\n\nThe more you work together, the more it becomes shaped by you.\n\n---\n\n## v1 vs v2: What Changed and Why\n\n### The Problem with v1\n\nv1 relied on **skills to observe**. Skills are probabilistic—they fire ~50-80% of the time based on Claude's judgment. This meant:\n\n- Session memory might not load\n- Patterns might not get detected\n- The homunculus often seemed \"dead\"\n\n### The v2 Solution\n\nv2 uses **hooks for observation** (100% reliable) and **instincts as the atomic unit** of learned behavior.\n\n| v1 | v2 |\n|----|-----|\n| Skills try to observe (unreliable) | **Hooks observe (100%)** |\n| Analysis in main context | **Analysis in background agent (Haiku)** |\n| Evolves big chunks (commands/skills) | **Evolves instincts first, clusters into bigger things** |\n| No sharing | **Export/import instincts** |\n\n### The Instinct Model\n\nAn **instinct** is a small learned behavior:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;yaml\n---\ntrigger: \"when writing new functions\"\nconfidence: 0.7\ndomain: \"code-style\"\n---\n\n# Prefer Functional Style\n\n## Action\nUse functional patterns over classes.\n\n## Evidence\nObserved 5 instances of functional pattern preference.\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nInstincts are:\n- **Atomic** — one trigger, one action\n- **Confidence-weighted** — 0.3 = tentative, 0.9 = near certain\n- **Domain-tagged** — code-style, testing, git, debugging, etc.\n\n### The Growth Path\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nSession Start\n     ↓\nObserver agent runs (background, Haiku)\n     ↓\nObservations → Instincts (auto-approved)\n     ↓\nInstincts cluster around a domain\n     ↓\nUser runs /homunculus:evolve\n     ↓\nBigger structures emerge:\n  - Command (if user-invoked)\n  - Skill (if auto-triggered)\n  - Agent (if needs isolation/depth)\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n**Fully automatic except evolution.** You just work. It learns.\n\n---\n\n## Architecture\n\n### File Structure\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nplugins/homunculus/\n├── .claude-plugin/\n│   └── plugin.json           # v2.0.0\n├── hooks/\n│   └── hooks.json            # Observation capture\n├── scripts/\n│   ├── observe.sh            # Captures prompts and tool use\n│   ├── on_stop.sh            # Updates session count\n│   └── test-homunculus.sh    # Test suite\n├── agents/\n│   └── observer.md           # Background analyzer (Haiku)\n├── skills/\n│   ├── session-memory/       # Spawns observer, loads context\n│   └── instinct-apply/       # Surfaces relevant instincts\n└── commands/\n    ├── init.md               # Birth/wake\n    ├── status.md             # Check in\n    ├── evolve.md             # Create capability from clusters\n    ├── export.md             # Share instincts\n    └── import.md             # Adopt instincts\n\nyour-project/.claude/homunculus/\n├── identity.json             # Who you are, your journey\n├── observations.jsonl        # Current session observations\n├── observations.archive.jsonl # Processed observations\n├── instincts/\n│   ├── personal/             # Learned instincts (auto-approved)\n│   └── inherited/            # Imported from others\n└── evolved/\n    ├── agents/               # Generated specialist agents\n    ├── skills/               # Generated skills\n    └── commands/             # Generated commands\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Data Flow\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nSession Start\n    ↓\nsession-memory skill activates\n    ├→ Spawn observer agent (background, Haiku)\n    ├→ Load identity + instincts\n    └→ Greet with context\n    ↓\nObserver Agent (parallel, silent)\n    ├→ Read observations.jsonl\n    ├→ Find patterns\n    ├→ Create instincts → personal/ (auto-approved)\n    ├→ Check clustering → flag in identity.json\n    └→ Archive observations\n    ↓\nUser Works\n    ↓\nHooks capture EVERYTHING\n    ├→ UserPromptSubmit → observations.jsonl\n    └→ PostToolUse → observations.jsonl\n    ↓\nSession End (Stop hook)\n    └→ Session count increments\n    ↓\nNext Session\n    └→ Observer processes new observations...\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n**Only manual step:** &#96;/homunculus:evolve&#96; when 5+ instincts cluster.\n\n---\n\n## Commands\n\n| Command | What It Does |\n|---------|--------------|\n| &#96;/homunculus:init&#96; | Birth or wake |\n| &#96;/homunculus:status&#96; | Check in (includes journey) |\n| &#96;/homunculus:evolve&#96; | Create capability from clustered instincts |\n| &#96;/homunculus:export&#96; | Share instincts |\n| &#96;/homunculus:import&#96; | Adopt instincts |\n\n**5 commands.** Everything else is automatic.\n\n---\n\n## Skills\n\n| Skill | When It Activates |\n|-------|-------------------|\n| &#96;session-memory&#96; | Session start — spawns observer, loads context |\n| &#96;instinct-apply&#96; | During work — surfaces relevant instincts |\n\n---\n\n## Reliability\n\n| What | Reliability |\n|------|-------------|\n| Observation capture (hooks) | **100%** |\n| Observer agent (session start) | **100%** |\n| Instinct creation | **100%** |\n| Commands | **100%** |\n| Skills (session-memory, instinct-apply) | ~50-80% |\n\nThe critical path is fully deterministic. Skills enhance the experience but aren't required.\n\n---\n\n## Instinct Domains\n\nInstincts are tagged with domains for clustering detection:\n\n| Domain | Examples |\n|--------|----------|\n| &#96;code-style&#96; | Functional patterns, naming conventions |\n| &#96;testing&#96; | Test-before-commit, coverage requirements |\n| &#96;git&#96; | Commit message format, branch naming |\n| &#96;debugging&#96; | Error→fix sequences, logging preferences |\n| &#96;file-organization&#96; | Where to put things |\n| &#96;tooling&#96; | Tool sequences, preferred tools |\n| &#96;communication&#96; | Comment style, documentation |\n\nWhen 5+ instincts accumulate in a domain, evolution can propose a specialist agent or skill.\n\n---\n\n## Sharing Instincts\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n# Export your instincts\n/homunculus:export\n# Creates .claude/homunculus/exports/instincts-TIMESTAMP.tar.gz\n\n# Import someone else's\n/homunculus:import path/to/instincts.tar.gz\n# Goes to inherited/, not personal/\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nInherited instincts are kept separate from personal ones. You can see where each behavior came from.\n\n---\n\n## Testing\n\nRun the test suite:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n./plugins/homunculus/scripts/test-homunculus.sh\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nTests cover:\n- Directory structure creation\n- Identity file validation\n- Observation capture\n- Instinct creation (auto-approved)\n- Session count updates\n- Instinct clustering detection\n- Evolution flag\n- Export functionality\n- Hook script execution\n- Plugin structure validation\n\n---\n\n## Adapts To You\n\nWhen you birth it, it asks how you work:\n\n**Technical** — Fast and sharp. No hand-holding.\n\n**Semi-technical** — A thinking partner.\n\n**Non-technical** — Focused on outcomes.\n\n**Chaotic** — Flows with whatever happens.\n\nSame creature, different personality.\n\n---\n\n## Make It Yours\n\nEverything is open. Fork it. Reshape its instincts. Change how it thinks.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\n# Edit the personality\nvim CLAUDE.md\n\n# Add a command\nvim plugins/homunculus/commands/my-thing.md\n\n# Add a skill\nmkdir plugins/homunculus/skills/my-skill\nvim plugins/homunculus/skills/my-skill/SKILL.md\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nThe homunculus you end up with should feel like something you grew—because you did.\n\n---\n\n## Landing Page\n\nThere's a landing page in &#96;/landing&#96; (React + Tailwind). Run it:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\ncd landing \u0026\u0026 npm install \u0026\u0026 npm run dev\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n---\n\nMIT License\n\n---\n\n*A small thing, growing toward the light.*\n",
  "url": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/humanplane/homunculus/main/README.md"
}

---

## GitHub 调研报告：AI Agent 自主学习/技能提取类工具

我系统搜索了 GitHub 上与"AI Agent 自主学习、知识提取、持久记忆"相关的项目，找到了 **6 个值得关注的项目**，按与我们项目的相关度排序：

---

### 1. Acontext — Agent Skills 作为记忆层（3,178 星）

**仓库**：[memodb-io/Acontext](https://github.com/memodb-io/Acontext)

**核心理念**：Skill 就是记忆，记忆就是 Skill。用 Markdown 文件存储记忆，不依赖向量数据库。

**工作方式**：
- 会话消息 → 任务完成/失败 → LLM 蒸馏（提取什么有效、什么失败、用户偏好）→ Skill Agent 决定存到哪个 Skill → 更新 Markdown 文件
- 召回时用 `get_skill` / `get_skill_file` 工具调用，不是语义搜索，是 **Agent 自己推理要什么**

**与我们的契合点**：
- "Skill = Memory" 的理念和我们的 "ABC 精炼 = 知识库" 高度一致
- 用 Markdown 文件存储、可导出 ZIP、无 vendor lock-in——和我们的 MD 主体 + JSON 镜像思路一致
- 支持自托管（Docker），也支持云端

**局限**：需要后端服务（PostgreSQL + Redis + RabbitMQ），架构偏重

---

### 2. agent-memory-skill — 基于遗忘曲线的三层记忆（MIT，零依赖）

**仓库**：[smixs/agent-memory-skill](https://github.com/smixs/agent-memory-skill)

**核心理念**：模拟人类记忆的遗忘曲线（Ebbinghaus），记忆随时间衰减，使用时增强。

**三层架构**：

| 层 | 内容 | 类比我们的 |
|---|---|---|
| Hot Context（<4KB） | 当前焦点、阻塞项 | 交接备忘 |
| Searchable Vault | 带 YAML frontmatter 的卡片，分 core/active/warm/cold/archive 五级 | 知识库层1（精炼 MD） |
| Archive | 旧日志、完成的项目 | 知识库层3（有价值全文） |

**亮点机制**：
- **relevance 衰减**：`relevance = max(0.1, 1.0 - days × 0.015)`，未使用的知识自动降级
- **Graduated Touch**：每次访问只升一级（archive→cold→warm→active），多次访问 = 更强记忆
- **Creative Mode**：随机从冷/归档层拉出卡片，模拟"灵光一闪"
- **零依赖**：一个 Python 文件，直接操作 Markdown 文件的 YAML frontmatter

**与我们的契合度极高**：
- 三层架构几乎和我们的知识库三层一一对应
- 纯 Markdown + YAML frontmatter，和 Obsidian 完美兼容
- 零依赖，不需要额外服务
- 遗忘曲线机制可以解决我们"知识库越来越大、什么都同等权重"的未来问题

---

### 3. Homunculus v2 — 本能式学习（Claude Code 插件）

**仓库**：[humanplane/homunculus](https://github.com/humanplane/homunculus)

**核心理念**：不直接提取 Skill，而是先学习"本能"（instinct）——原子级行为规则，积累到 5+ 条同领域本能后再聚合为 Skill/Command/Agent。

**关键设计**：
- **Hook 观察（100% 可靠）**：用 `UserPromptSubmit` + `PostToolUse` Hook 捕获所有交互，不依赖 Skill 的概率性触发
- **本能模型**：trigger + action + confidence（0.3~0.9）+ domain 标签
- **后台分析**：用 Haiku（廉价模型）在后台静默分析，不占主上下文
- **进化路径**：观察 → 本能（自动）→ 聚类 → 用户手动 `/evolve` → 生成 Skill/Command/Agent

**与我们的借鉴点**：
- "本能 → 聚类 → Skill" 的渐进式学习，比直接提取 Skill 更稳健——类似我们的"积累够了再写入核心画像"
- confidence 衰减机制（被反驳则降低置信度）——和我们的交叉验证逻辑一致
- domain 标签分类——和我们的 ABC 分类思路一致

**局限**：Claude Code 专属插件，不适用于 Cursor

---

### 4. claude-code-semantic-memory — 语义记忆系统（74 星）

**仓库**：[zacdcook/claude-code-semantic-memory](https://github.com/zacdcook/claude-code-semantic-memory)

**核心理念**：把 `.jsonl` 会话记录转为 Markdown → 用子 Agent 提取 learnings → 本地嵌入模型（nomic-embed-text）做向量化 → 每次 prompt 时注入相关记忆。

**学习类型分类**：

| 类型 | 说明 | 类比我们的 |
|---|---|---|
| WORKING_SOLUTION | 确认有效的命令/模式 | B 知识 |
| GOTCHA | 陷阱和反直觉行为 | B 知识 |
| PATTERN | 反复出现的架构决策 | B 知识 |
| DECISION | 明确的设计选择+理由 | B 知识 / C 用户观点 |
| FAILURE | 什么没用+为什么 | B 知识（教训） |
| PREFERENCE | 用户偏好 | A 画像 |

**与我们的借鉴点**：
- 学习类型分类和我们的 ABC 有对应关系，可以参考其分类粒度
- "Hook 优于 CLAUDE.md"的设计决策有道理——Hook 确定性触发，CLAUDE.md 指令在认知负载高时可能被跳过
- 上下文压缩时自动导出+提取的机制值得参考

**局限**：需要 Ollama 本地嵌入服务，Claude Code 专属

---

### 5. Claudeception — 自主技能提取（2,137 星）

**仓库**：[blader/Claudeception](https://github.com/blader/Claudeception)（上一轮已详细调研）

已在上一轮分析过，不重复。

---

### 6. continuous-learning（affaan-m/everything-claude-code）

**核心**：Stop Hook 在会话结束时评估，提取 5 类模式（error_resolution / user_corrections / workarounds / debugging_techniques / project_specific）。

**特点**：轻量，一个 Shell 脚本 + config.json，但只在会话结束时跑一次，粒度较粗。

---

## 碎片整合

你的意思是：**让我去 GitHub 上系统搜索类似 Claudeception 的 SK/自主学习工具，看看有哪些值得借鉴的理念和机制，最终目的是改进我们数字分身项目的知识沉淀流程**。

| 类型 | 内容 |
|------|------|
| 🔵 事实 | 找到 6 个相关项目，覆盖了从轻量（零依赖单文件）到重量（需要后端服务）的完整光谱 |
| 🟡 推断 | 你最关心的是"理念和机制能不能用"，不是"直接安装哪个" |
| 🔴 待确认 | 你想让我进一步做什么——(a) 出一份"哪些机制可以移植到我们项目"的具体方案？(b) 直接挑一个最合适的试装？(c) 先汇报给大脑窗口决策？ |

**理解对吗？**

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="run_terminal_command_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: # D盘按分钟级时间戳分组，看有多少组是A+B共存
$files = Get-ChildItem "D:\数字分身2\.specstory\history" -Filter "*.md"
$groups = @{}
foreach ($f in $files) {
    if ($f.Name -match '^(\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}_\d{2}-\d{2})') {
        $key = $Matches[1]
        if (-not $groups[$key]) { $groups[$key] = @() }
        $groups[$key] += $f.Name
    }
}
$dupGroups = 0
$totalB = 0
foreach ($key in ($groups.Keys | Sort-Object)) {
    $g = $groups[$key]
    $hasA = $g | Where-Object { $_ -match "^$($key)Z" }
    $hasB = $g | Where-Object { $_ -match "^$($key)-\d{2}Z" }
    if ($hasA -and $hasB) {
        $dupGroups++
        $totalB += @($hasB).Count
        Write-Host "[$key] A=$(@($hasA).Count) B=$(@($hasB).Count) | A: $($hasA[0]) | B: $($hasB[0])"
    }
}
Write-Host "`n共 $dupGroups 组A+B共存, 可删除 $totalB 个File B"</summary>

```bash
# D盘按分钟级时间戳分组，看有多少组是A+B共存
$files = Get-ChildItem "D:\数字分身2\.specstory\history" -Filter "*.md"
$groups = @{}
foreach ($f in $files) {
    if ($f.Name -match '^(\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}_\d{2}-\d{2})') {
        $key = $Matches[1]
        if (-not $groups[$key]) { $groups[$key] = @() }
        $groups[$key] += $f.Name
    }
}
$dupGroups = 0
$totalB = 0
foreach ($key in ($groups.Keys | Sort-Object)) {
    $g = $groups[$key]
    $hasA = $g | Where-Object { $_ -match "^$($key)Z" }
    $hasB = $g | Where-Object { $_ -match "^$($key)-\d{2}Z" }
    if ($hasA -and $hasB) {
        $dupGroups++
        $totalB += @($hasB).Count
        Write-Host "[$key] A=$(@($hasA).Count) B=$(@($hasB).Count) | A: $($hasA[0]) | B: $($hasB[0])"
    }
}
Write-Host "`n共 $dupGroups 组A+B共存, 可删除 $totalB 个File B"
```

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="run_terminal_command_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: python -c "
import os, re
from collections import defaultdict

DIR = r'E:\数字\vault\数字分身\待确认'
files = [f for f in os.listdir(DIR) if f.endswith('.md')]

# 按分钟级时间戳分组
groups = defaultdict(list)
for f in files:
    core = re.sub(r'^\[待确认\]\s*', '', f)
    # 提取分钟级时间戳
    m = re.match(r'(\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}_\d{2}-\d{2})', core)
    if m:
        groups[m.group(1)].append(f)

# 找出同一分钟有多个文件的组
print(f'待确认文件总数: {len(files)}')
print(f'分钟级时间戳分组数: {len(groups)}')
print()

multi = {k: v for k, v in groups.items() if len(v) > 1}
print(f'同一分钟有多个文件的组: {len(multi)}')
for ts in sorted(multi.keys()):
    flist = multi[ts]
    print(f'\n[{ts}] ({len(flist)} 个文件):')
    for f in sorted(flist):
        size = os.path.getsize(os.path.join(DIR, f))
        print(f'  {f}  ({size} bytes)')
"
</summary>

```bash
python -c "
import os, re
from collections import defaultdict

DIR = r'E:\数字\vault\数字分身\待确认'
files = [f for f in os.listdir(DIR) if f.endswith('.md')]

# 按分钟级时间戳分组
groups = defaultdict(list)
for f in files:
    core = re.sub(r'^\[待确认\]\s*', '', f)
    # 提取分钟级时间戳
    m = re.match(r'(\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}_\d{2}-\d{2})', core)
    if m:
        groups[m.group(1)].append(f)

# 找出同一分钟有多个文件的组
print(f'待确认文件总数: {len(files)}')
print(f'分钟级时间戳分组数: {len(groups)}')
print()

multi = {k: v for k, v in groups.items() if len(v) > 1}
print(f'同一分钟有多个文件的组: {len(multi)}')
for ts in sorted(multi.keys()):
    flist = multi[ts]
    print(f'\n[{ts}] ({len(flist)} 个文件):')
    for f in sorted(flist):
        size = os.path.getsize(os.path.join(DIR, f))

import os, re

DIR = r'E:\数字\vault\数字分身\待确认'
